name: Ramda
what_it_does: Practical functional programming library for JavaScript with automatic currying, function composition, and immutable data operations. Ramda's functions take data as the last argument (enabling partial application) and never mutate inputs. The go-to library for point-free functional style in JavaScript — R.pipe, R.compose, R.map, R.filter, R.reduce, R.lens. Alternative to Lodash with stronger functional discipline.
use_cases: Build composable data transformation pipelines for agent output processing using R.pipe/R.compose for readable function chains
Transform deeply nested JSON structures using Ramda's lens API without mutation — useful for agent config and state manipulation
Process collections functionally in agent data pipelines with R.map, R.filter, R.reduce, R.groupBy without side effects
Create partially applied utility functions for agent workflows using Ramda's automatic currying (R.filter(isActive) returns a function)
Implement point-free data processing pipelines where the data argument is implicit and functions are composed rather than called
not_for: Teams not familiar with functional programming — Ramda's point-free style and currying can be confusing for developers coming from imperative backgrounds
Performance-critical hot paths — Ramda's currying and immutability have overhead vs native array methods or Lodash in tight loops
Simple data manipulation — Lodash is more intuitive and widely known for straightforward array/object operations
best_when: Your team embraces functional programming and wants automatic currying, immutability, and composition-first data transformation without mutation.
avoid_when: Your team is more comfortable with Lodash-style imperative utilities or you need maximum performance in hot paths.

pricing.notes: Ramda is open source and free.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Ramda functions fail silently for wrong types (returning null/undefined) rather than throwing — type errors surface as undefined behavior downstream rather than clear error messages. TypeScript types help but are incomplete.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: All Ramda functions are pure and immutable — no side effects, same input always produces same output.
known_agent_gotchas: Ramda functions expect data as the last argument — R.map(fn, list) but also R.map(fn)(list); this is opposite to Lodash which puts data first
R.equals does deep structural equality but doesn't handle circular references — use with caution on deeply nested or recursive agent data structures
Ramda's TypeScript types are available via @types/ramda but are incomplete for complex composition scenarios — type errors in R.pipe chains can be cryptic
R.curry wraps a function but arity must be known — variadic functions (rest params) don't curry correctly; use R.curryN to specify arity explicitly
Lens operations (R.view, R.set, R.over) don't support array index creation — setting an index beyond array length doesn't work like direct array assignment
Tree-shaking Ramda requires explicit per-function imports (import map from 'ramda/src/map') — default import includes the full 250+ function library

security_score_components.security_notes: Pure functional library with no side effects. No network access. No security concerns beyond general JavaScript security practices.
